docs.title: Unnameable
docs.text:
A procedural, statically compiled programming language for Linux x86-64.

Unnameable compiles directly to native binaries via an LLVM backend with no dependency on the C runtime — the language ships its own minimal runtime instead.

Source files use the .unn extension. The compiler binary is unnc.

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
func main:i32 {
    trace "Hello World"
    return 0
}

Use the navigation to explore the language. Start with Getting Started if you're new.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Linux x86-64
  • LLVM 18.1.x (llvm-config, ld.lld)

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Build instructions coming soon.

docs.title: Your first program
docs.text:
Create a file called main.unn:

func main:i32 {
    trace "Hello World"
    return 0
}

Compile and run:

unnc main.unn
./main

Output: Hello World

docs.title: Compiler flags
docs.text: Flag Description -build <n> Compile and link to executable -compile <file> Compile to object file only -static Generate a static library -stub <file> Generate a stub file -check <file> Run the front end only (no codegen) -verbose Enable verbose internal logs

docs.title: Optimization levels
docs.text: Flag Description --debug No optimizations, fastest compile --basic Basic optimizations --release Standard optimizations (recommended) --aggressive Aggressive optimizations --size Optimize for binary size

docs.title: Variables and mutability
docs.text:
By default all variables in Unnameable are immutable once assigned. To allow reassignment you must explicitly mark a variable with mut:

i32 x = 10        
mut i32 y = 10    
y = 20

For values known at compile time you can use const:

const i32 MAX = 100

const requires a compile-time literal. Runtime values are not allowed. Currently const is only supported for scalar types.

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
Unnameable is statically typed. Every variable declaration follows this general shape:

[modifiers] type name = value

docs.title: Scalar types
docs.text:
Type Description i8 8-bit signed integer i16 16-bit signed integer i32 32-bit signed integer i64 64-bit signed integer i128 128-bit signed integer u8 8-bit unsigned integer u16 16-bit unsigned integer u32 32-bit unsigned integer u64 64-bit unsigned integer u128 128-bit unsigned integer usize Unsigned pointer-sized integer (native width) isize Signed pointer-sized integer (native width) char8 8-bit character char16 16-bit character char32 32-bit character string String type f32 32-bit floating point f64 64-bit floating point

User-defined types are also supported. The compiler has no naming restrictions on custom types — any identifier that does not collide with a reserved keyword is valid.

docs.title: Type inference
docs.text:
If you don't want to write the type explicitly you can use auto. The compiler infers the type from the initializer — an initializer is required:

auto y = 100

auto only infers the base type. Other modifiers such as mut, heap, and const must still be written explicitly:

mut heap auto x = some_value

docs.title: Pointers
docs.text:
A pointer is declared using the ptr modifier:

ptr i32 p

Pointers are bound using -> with addr to take the address of a variable:

i32 x = 10
ptr i32 p -> addr x

Pointers can be nested to any depth:

ptr<ptr> i32 p
ptr<ptr<ptr>> i32 p

docs.title: Opaque pointers
docs.text:
opaque is a typeless pointer, the equivalent of void* in C. It must always appear behind a pointer modifier and cannot be dereferenced:

ptr opaque p

docs.title: References
docs.text:
A reference is an alias — another name for the same variable with no memory of its own. Mutating through a reference mutates the original:

mut heap i32 x = 100
ref i32 rx -> x
rx = 200

References inherit mutability from the variable they are bound to. Writing mut ref or const ref is silently ignored by the compiler.

References can only be bound to heap or global variables. This restriction exists to prevent dangling references.

They can be passed to and accepted by functions:

func ref_test(ref i32 r):void {
    trace r
}

func main:i32 {
    mut heap i32 x = 100
    ref i32 rx -> x
    ref_test(rx)
    return 0
}

docs.title: Arrays
docs.text:
Arrays require either an explicit size or an initializer list:

arr[2] i32 x
arr i32 x = [1, 2]

Multidimensional arrays are expressed by nesting array modifiers:

arr[2]<arr[3]> i32 x

docs.title: Mixing modifiers
docs.text:
Modifiers can be combined. For example a pointer to an array of two i32 values:

ptr<arr[2]> i32 x

docs.title: Heap allocation
docs.text:
Adding the heap modifier places a variable on the heap and hands memory management to the compiler's built-in allocator:

heap i32 x = 10

Without heap the compiler makes no assumptions about memory — you are responsible. The details of heap allocation and custom allocators are covered in their own section.

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Functions are declared with func, followed by the name, optional parameters, and a return type after the colon:

func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
    return a + b
}

func main:i32 {
    i32 result = add(10, 12)
    trace result
    return 0
}

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The trace keyword prints a value to stdout:

trace "Hello World"
trace result

docs.title: Comments
docs.text:
Single line comments use #:

# This is a single line comment
i32 x = 10  # Inline comment

Multiline comments are wrapped in ##:

## 
This is a 
multiline comment 
##

docs.title: Globals
docs.text: Variables declared outside any function are global. They follow the same declaration syntax as local variables with a few restrictions.

docs.title: Basic globals
docs.text:
const i32 MAX_VALUE = 100
i32 my_var = 88
mut i32 another_var = 87
i32 uninit

Uninitialized globals are zero-initialized by the compiler. const globals must be compile-time literals.

docs.title: Pointers and references at global scope
docs.text:
Globals can be pointers or references bound to other globals:

i32 x = 188
ptr i32 px -> addr x
ref i32 rx -> x

docs.title: Arrays at global scope
docs.text:
Arrays are allowed at global scope. The size must be a compile-time constant or inferred from an initializer:

arr[3] i32 nums
arr i32 primes = [2, 3, 5, 7]

docs.title: Initializers
docs.text:
Global initializers must be compile-time literals or other globals. Runtime expressions are not valid at global scope:

i32 x = 100
i32 y = x

docs.title: Rules and restrictions
docs.text:
• Assignments are not allowed at global scope — only inside functions
  • heap declarations are not allowed at global scope
  • Function calls are not allowed at global scope
  • Globals cannot be marked export

docs.title: Type casting
docs.text:
Unnameable has two casting operators — cast and bitcast. Both use the same syntax:

operator<dest_type>(src)

docs.title: cast
docs.text:
cast is the safe casting operator. It works on scalar types only:

cast<i32>(x)
cast<f64>(x)

Custom types are not valid destinations for cast:

cast<MyType>(x)

docs.title: bitcast
docs.text:
bitcast is a raw reinterpretation of the underlying bits. It is more powerful than cast and works on pointers and custom types behind a pointer:

bitcast<ptr i32>(x)
bitcast<ptr MyType>(x)

Use bitcast carefully — it bypasses type safety entirely and reinterprets memory as-is.

docs.title: If, elif, else
docs.text:
func if_test(i32 val):void {
    if(val == 1) {
        trace 1
    } elif(val == 2) {
        trace 2
    } else {
        trace "I dont know"
    }
}

Conditions are wrapped in parentheses. elif is used for additional branches rather than else if.

docs.title: While loop
docs.text:
func while_test:void {
    mut i32 x = 1
    while(x < 3) {
        x++
        trace x
    }
}

docs.title: For loop
docs.text:
func for_test:void {
    for(mut i32 i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
        trace i
    }
}

The loop variable is declared inline with a full type declaration. The variable must be mut since the loop increments it.

docs.title: Switch
docs.text:
func switch_test(i32 val):void {
    switch(val) {
        case 1:
            trace 1
        case 2:
            trace 2
        default:
            trace "No Num"
    }
}

Unlike C, the compiler always breaks after each case. Fallthrough is not possible as each case is isolated by design.

docs.title: Declaration
docs.text:
Functions are declared with func, followed by the name, optional parameters, and a mandatory return type after the colon:

func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
    return a + b
}

The return type is not optional. Functions that return nothing must explicitly declare :void:

func greet:void {
    trace "Hello"
}

All functions must exist at global scope unless they are inside a seal or a component.

docs.title: Calling functions
docs.text:
func main:i32 {
    i32 result = add(10, 12)
    trace result
    return 0
}

docs.title: Forward declarations
docs.text:
A function can be declared without a body by omitting it entirely. The same syntax rules apply — return type is mandatory, parameters follow the same form:

func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32

There are two use cases:

Local forward declaration — the implementation exists later in the same file. Do not mark it export:

func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32

func main:i32 {
    trace add(10, 12)
    return 0
}

func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
    return a + b
}

External declaration — the implementation lives in another file or library. Mark it export to tell the linker to find it externally:

export func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32

Omitting export on an external declaration will result in a linker error — the compiler will look for the implementation in the same file and not find it.

Seals only accept function definitions. Forward declarations cannot be placed inside a seal.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• No multiple return values
  • No variadic functions
  • No function overloading — each function name must be unique in its scope
  • No higher order functions
  • No function pointers (planned)

docs.title: The export modifier
docs.text:
export is a function modifier that does three things at once:

  1. Exposes the function to external linkage
  2. Makes the function available to the stub system
  3. Serializes its signature into binary format for modular use
export func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
    return a + b
}

docs.title: Seals
docs.text:
A seal is a function namespace. It groups related functions together and prevents name collisions:

seal Maths {
    func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
        return a + b
    }

    func subtract(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
        return a - b
    }
}

Functions inside a seal are called using dot notation:

func main:i32 {
    trace "Sealed Add res:", Maths.add(19, 7)
    trace "Sub res:", Maths.subtract(7, 8)
    return 0
}

A sealed function and a global function can share the same name without conflict:

seal Maths {
    func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
        return a + b
    }
}

func add(i32 x, i32 y):i32 {
    return x + y
}

func main:i32 {
    trace "Global:", add(77, 88)
    trace "Sealed:", Maths.add(19, 7)
    return 0
}

docs.title: Name mangling
docs.text: The compiler mangles all function names inside a seal regardless of export status. A function add inside seal Maths becomes Maths_add at the linker level. This is how the compiler guarantees no collisions between sealed and global functions.

docs.title: Exporting seals
docs.text:
You can apply export to an entire seal, which exports all functions inside it at once:

export seal Maths {
    func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
        return a + b
    }

    func subtract(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
        return a - b
    }
}

Or you can target specific functions inside a seal while leaving the rest unexported:

seal Maths {
    export func add(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
        return a + b
    }

    func subtract(i32 a, i32 b):i32 {
        return a - b
    }
}

In both cases name mangling still applies to all functions in the seal.

docs.title: trace
docs.text:
trace prints values to stdout. It accepts multiple arguments separated by commas — each argument is printed on its own line:

trace "Hello World"
trace x, y, z
trace "Result:", result

Note: when passing a label and a value as separate arguments they will each print on their own line.

docs.title: Arithmetic operators
docs.text: Operator Description + Addition - Subtraction * Multiplication / Division % Modulo

docs.title: Comparison operators
docs.text: Operator Description == Equal != Not equal < Less than > Greater than <= Less than or equal >= Greater than or equal

docs.title: Logical operators
docs.text: Operator Description && Logical AND \|\| Logical OR ! Logical NOT

docs.title: Bitwise operators
docs.text: Operator Description & Bitwise AND \| Bitwise OR ^ Bitwise XOR ~ Bitwise NOT << Left shift >> Right shift

docs.title: Compound assignment
docs.text:
Unnameable does not have compound assignment operators. +=, -=, and similar are not supported — write the full expression instead:

x = x + 1
x = x - 1

docs.title: Increment and decrement
docs.text:
Both prefix and postfix forms are supported with the same semantics as C:

x++    
x--    
++x    
--x

docs.title: addr
docs.text:
addr takes the address of a variable — equivalent to & in C:

i32 x = 10
ptr i32 p -> addr x

addr can only be applied to a named variable in memory. The following are illegal:

addr call()        
addr 42            
addr (x + y)

docs.title: deref
docs.text:
deref dereferences a pointer — equivalent to * in C:

i32 val = deref p

Nesting is allowed for multiple levels of indirection:

i32 val = deref deref p

docs.title: The binding operator ->
docs.text:
-> is used exclusively when assigning to a pointer or reference — both at declaration and reassignment. Using = for pointer binding is not allowed:

i32 x = 10
ptr i32 p -> addr x

i32 y = 20
p -> addr y

docs.title: Nullability
docs.text:
Unnameable has a first-class nullability system. By default all types are non-nullable — a variable cannot hold null unless its type is explicitly marked nullable with ?:

i32? x = null

Nullable types work with all modifiers:

ptr i32? p
arr[3] i32? x

docs.title: Passing nullable values
docs.text:
A nullable type cannot be passed to a function expecting a non-nullable type. The compiler enforces this strictly — you must resolve the nullability before passing:

func takes_int(i32 val):void {
    trace val
}

i32? x = 10
i32 safe = unwrap x
takes_int(safe)

Passing x directly to takes_int is illegal — unwrap it into a non-nullable variable first.

docs.title: Nullable return types
docs.text:
Functions can return nullable types:

func maybe_value(i32 input):i32? {
    if(input == 0) {
        return null
    }
    return input
}

func main:i32 {
    i32? result = maybe_value(0)
    trace result ?? -1
    return 0
}

docs.title: The coalesce operator ??
docs.text:
?? returns the value if it is not null, or a fallback if it is:

func main:i32 {
    i32? x = null
    trace x ?? 5      
    return 0
}

If x holds a value that value is returned instead:

i32? x = 10
trace x ?? 5

docs.title: unwrap
docs.text:
unwrap extracts the value from a nullable type. If the value is null the program traps immediately:

i32? x = null
trace unwrap x

To use the value safely unwrap it into a non-nullable variable first. If unwrapping succeeds the resulting variable is clean and can be used freely:

i32? x = 10
i32 safe = unwrap x
trace safe

If x is null the trap fires at the unwrap line — safe is never assigned.

docs.title: Records
docs.text:
A record is Unnameable's struct equivalent — a named collection of fields with no methods.

record Rect {
    i32 x
    i32 y
}

Fields are immutable by default. To make all fields mutable mark the record with mut:

mut record Random {
    i32 x = 77
    i32 y = 78
}

Fields can carry default values. Without defaults the compiler zero-initializes.

docs.title: Volatile records
docs.text:
volatile tells the compiler not to optimize away reads and writes to the record's fields — useful for memory-mapped hardware registers or shared memory:

volatile record Random {
    i32 x = 77
    i32 y = 78
}

This behaves the same as volatile in C.

docs.title: Instantiation
docs.text:
There are exactly two ways to instantiate a record:

Rect rectangle = Rect{x=100, y=50}

Rect rectangle

The first form initializes fields explicitly. The second default-initializes — zeros unless the record declared default values.

docs.title: Passing records to functions
docs.text:
Records can be passed to and returned from functions. Fields are accessed with dot notation:

record Bounds {
    i32 width
    i32 height
}

record Rect {
    i32 x
    i32 y
}

func dimensions(Rect r):Bounds {
    trace r.x
    return Bounds{width=r.x, height=r.y}
}

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• No methods
  • No nesting — a record cannot contain another record directly
  • No heap on individual fields — storage is tied to the instance

docs.title: Export
docs.text:
Records can be marked export to expose them to external linkage and the stub system:

export record Rect {
    i32 x
    i32 y
}

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
Enums in Unnameable are scoped — variants cannot leak into the surrounding namespace. They only work on integer types.

enum HttpStatus {
    OK = 200,
    NotFound = 404,
    InternalServerError = 500
}

The default underlying type is i32. To specify a different integer type declare it after the enum name:

enum HttpStatus:u16 {
    OK = 200u16,
    NotFound = 404u16,
    InternalServerError = 500u16
}

docs.title: Accessing variants
docs.text:
Variants are accessed using :: notation:

auto code = HttpStatus::OK

docs.title: Using enums in functions
docs.text:
func get_http_code(HttpStatus status):i32 {
    if(status == HttpStatus::OK) {
        return 200
    } elif(status == HttpStatus::NotFound) {
        return 404
    } elif(status == HttpStatus::InternalServerError) {
        return 500
    }
    return 0
}

func main:i32 {
    auto code = HttpStatus::OK
    i32 raw_code = get_http_code(code)
    trace raw_code
    return 0
}

docs.title: Export
docs.text:
Enums can be marked export to expose them to the stub system:

export enum HttpStatus {
    OK = 200,
    NotFound = 404,
    InternalServerError = 500
}

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
A component is a record with additional capabilities — methods, field injection, an optional init constructor, and access to self.

component Player {
    i32 speed
    i32 strength

    func get_player_speed():i32 {
        return self.speed
    }

    func buff_player:void {
        self.speed = self.speed + 10
        self.strength = self.strength + 10
    }

    init(i32 player_speed, i32 player_strength) {
        self.speed = player_speed
        self.strength = player_strength
    }
}

docs.title: Self
docs.text:
Inside a method, fields and other methods are accessible via self:

return self.speed

self. is optional — the compiler will resolve bare field names inside a method automatically. Both forms are valid:

return self.speed
return speed

docs.title: The init constructor
docs.text:
init is a special function that lets you construct a component with custom starting values. It always returns void and is not mandatory — if you don't define one, the new initialization syntax is unavailable.

init(i32 player_speed, i32 player_strength) {
    self.speed = player_speed
    self.strength = player_strength
}

docs.title: Instantiation
docs.text:
If the component has an init:

Player player_0 = new Player(100, 78)

Despite the new keyword this is stack allocated. For heap allocation:

heap Player player_0 = new Player(100, 78)

Default initialization (zeros or declared defaults):

Player player_1
heap Player player_1

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
Methods follow the same syntax as global functions. They live inside the component body and have access to self:

func get_player_speed():i32 {
    return self.speed
}

docs.title: Field injection
docs.text:
Injection pulls all fields from a record directly into the component at the frontend — no extra indirection involved:

record Buffs {
    i32 super_speed = 10
    i32 super_strength = 10
}

component Player {
    i32 speed
    i32 strength

    inject record Buffs

    func buff_player:void {
        self.speed = self.speed + self.super_speed
        self.strength = self.strength + self.super_strength
    }
}

After injection super_speed and super_strength become direct fields of Player as if they were declared there.

To inject a single field only use @:

inject record Buffs@super_speed

Warning: The compiler treats field name collisions as a hard error regardless of which records the fields came from. If two injected records share a field name the compiler will reject it. To avoid this embed the record as a field instead — Buffs buffs — and access it via dot notation.

docs.title: Only records can be injected
docs.text: You cannot inject from another component — only records are valid injection sources.

docs.title: Export
docs.text:
Marking a component export exposes it and all its methods to external linkage and the stub system:

export component Player {
    ...
}

Individual methods can also be targeted:

component Player {
    export func get_player_speed():i32 {
        return self.speed
    }

    func buff_player:void {
        ...
    }
}
